request.question: What was the name of the 1929 novel written by the author whose scenario inspired the one-act ballet "Le Train Bleu"?

title: Le Train Bleu (restaurant)
Blue Train Races
Bar aux Folies-Bergère (ballet)
Picasso and the Ballets Russes
Le Train de Nulle Part
Jean Cocteau
Pep Bertran
Hidden Agenda (video game)
Le Train Bleu
Le Train Bleu (ballet)
sentences: Le Train Bleu ("The Blue Train") is a restaurant located in the hall of the Gare de Lyon railway station in Paris, France.
It was designated a "Monument Historique" in 1972.
The Blue Train Races were a series of record-breaking attempts between automobiles and trains in the late 1920s and early 1930s.
It saw a number of motorists and their own or sponsored automobiles race against the "Le Train Bleu", a train that ran between Calais and the French Riviera.

The rationale to beat the train was to compare the contemporary automotive performance with locomotive dominance; to showcase recent progress achieved by cars regarding reliability, durability, speed and comfort; to promote the cars, their marques and the adventurous persona of their drivers; and to establish automobiles as a viable and aspirational mode of transport for the individual traveller.
Bar aux Folies-Bergère is a one-act ballet created in 1934, with scenario and choreography by Ninette de Valois, and designs by William Chappell after Manet.
The music consists of piano works by Emmanuel Chabrier, selected and arranged by Constant Lambert.
Pablo Picasso and the Ballets Russes collaborated on several productions.

Pablo Picasso's Cubist sets and costumes were used by Sergei Diaghilev in the Ballets Russes's "Parade" (1917, choreography: Léonide Massine), "Le Tricorne" ("The Three-Cornered Hat") (1919, choreography: Massine), "Pulcinella" (1920, choreographer: Massine), and "Cuadro Flamenco" (1921, choreography: Spanish folk dancers).
Picasso also drew a sketch with pen on paper of "La Boutique fantasque" ("La course"), (1919, choreography: Massine).
and designed the drop curtain for "Le Train Bleu" (1924, choreography: Bronislava Nijinska), based on his painting, "Two Women Running on the Beach" ("The Race"), 1922.
Le Train de Nulle Part (The Train from Nowhere) is a 233-page French novel, written in 2004 by a French doctor of letters, Michel Dansel, under the pen name Michel Thaler.
Notable as an example of constrained writing, the entire novel is written without a single verb.
Jean Maurice Eugène Clément Cocteau (] ; 5 July 1889 – 11 October 1963) was a French writer, designer, playwright, artist and filmmaker.
Cocteau is best known for his novel "Les Enfants Terribles" (1929), and the films "The Blood of a Poet" (1930), "Les Parents Terribles" (1948), "Beauty and the Beast" (1946) and "Orpheus" (1949).

His circle of associates, friends and lovers included Kenneth Anger, Pablo Picasso, Gertrude Stein, Jean Hugo, Jean Marais, Henri Bernstein, Yul Brynner, Marlene Dietrich, Coco Chanel, Erik Satie, Albert Gleizes, Igor Stravinsky, Marie Laurencin, María Félix, Édith Piaf, Panama Al Brown, Colette, Jean Genet, and Raymond Radiguet.
Pep Bertran (born in Mataró, Maresme, 20 September 1971) is a Catalan writer.
He studied information science, psychoanalysis and film direction.
In 1996 he received the "Premi de novel·la curta Just Manuel Casero" Award for his novel "La Ribera", which was published in 1997 by Empúries publishing house.
Then, he left the writing and he was trained as a psychoanalyst.
He collaborated on "El Punt" newspaper between 1997 and 1998.
His novel "Una vida regalada", the first of the "Narratives" collection (Edicions Saldonar) is a fake autobiography of author whose scenario is La Ribera of Barcelona.

Hidden Agenda is a 1988 computer strategy game with menu-driven interface and early, black and white graphics whose scenario was designed and written by Jim Gasperini, with input from project consultant Eric Ehrmann.
While the scenario was implemented in text form, the game made use of an innovative graphical interface, with naturalistic characters, settings, and digital video interstitials.
The narrative simulation system was designed and implemented by Greg Guerin and Ron Martinez (who also produced the game).
Ron Martinez also designed and implemented the front end user experience, in the process inventing one of the first implementations of digital video with frames grabbed from a hacked four-head VCR.
The game was intended to simulate the conditions of a post-revolutionary Central American country.
It is considered a forerunner of the Games for Change movement, alongside other early Macintosh games including Chris Crawford's "Balance of Power".
The Calais-Mediterranée Express was a luxury French night express train which operated from 1886 to 2003.
It gained international fame as the preferred train of wealthy and famous passengers between Calais and the French Riviera in the two decades before World War II.
It was colloquially referred to as Le Train Bleu in French (which became its formal name after World War II) and the Blue Train in English because of its dark blue sleeping cars.
Le train bleu is a one-act ballet choreographed by Bronislava Nijinska to music by Darius Milhaud for Serge Diaghilev's Ballets Russes, based on a scenario by Jean Cocteau.
The title was taken from the night train called Le Train Bleu, which transported wealthy passengers from Calais to the Mediterranean Sea.
The ballet is set at the fashionable resort of Deauville and has a sporting theme, with swimmers, tennis players, and weight lifters.
Henri Laurens supplied a Cubist beach scene and Coco Chanel outfitted the cast in sportswear.
The curtain was painted after "Deux femmes courant sur la plage", a 1922 work by Pablo Picasso.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: What was the name of the 1929 novel written by the author whose scenario inspired the one-act ballet "Le Train Bleu"?
hop1_docs: Le Train Bleu (ballet) | Le train bleu is a one-act ballet choreographed by Bronislava Nijinska to music by Darius Milhaud for Serge Diaghilev's Ballets Russes, based on a scenario by Jean Cocteau.  The title was taken from the night train called Le Train Bleu, which transported wealthy passengers from Calais to the Mediterranean Sea.  The ballet is set at the fashionable resort of Deauville and has a sporting theme, with swimmers, tennis players, and weight lifters.  Henri Laurens supplied a Cubist beach scene and Coco Chanel outfitted the cast in sportswear.  The curtain was painted after "Deux femmes courant sur la plage", a 1922 work by Pablo Picasso.

Le Dieu bleu | Le Dieu bleu is a ballet in one act choreographed by Michel Fokine to music by Reynaldo Hahn, set to a libretto by Jean Cocteau and Federico de Madrazo y Ochoa.  Léon Bakst designed the sets and costumes.  The ballet was a failure at the premiere in Paris, on 13 May 1912 at the Théâtre du Châtelet.  "Le Dieu bleu" was staged three times in Paris in 1912, three times in London in 1913, and revived in London in April 2011 with music cobbled together from the works of Scriabin and with choreography by Wayne Eagling.  The revival was not a success.

The Gas Heart | The Gas Heart or The Gas-Operated Heart (French: "Le Cœur à gaz" ) is a French-language play by Romanian-born author Tristan Tzara.  It was written as a series of non sequiturs and a parody of classical drama—it has three acts despite being short enough to qualify as a one-act play.  A part-musical performance that features ballet numbers, it is one of the most recognizable plays inspired by the anti-establishment trend known as Dadaism.  "The Gas Heart" was first staged in Paris, as part of the 1921 "Dada Salon" at the Galerie Montaigne.

L'Oiseau Bleu (train) | L'Oiseau Bleu (or the Oiseau Bleu) was an international express train linking Antwerp with Paris.  The train was named after the play "L'Oiseau Bleu" as a tribute to its author, the Belgian Nobel prize laureate Maurice Maeterlinck.

The Blue Dahlia (ballet) | The Blue Dahlia (French: "Le Dahlia bleu" ) is a fantastic ballet in two acts, with libretto and choreography by Marius Petipa and music by Cesare Pugni, first presented by the Imperial Ballet on May 12 [O.S. April 30] 1860 at the Imperial Bolshoi Kamenny Theatre in St. Petersburg, Russia, with Mariia Surovshchikova-Petipa (as the Blue Dahlia) and Timofei Stukolkin (as Beausoleil).

Le Pavillon d'Armide | Le Pavillon d'Armide is a ballet in one act and three scenes choreographed by Michel Fokine with music by Nikolai Tcherepnin on a libretto by Alexandre Benois.  It was inspired by the novella "Omphale" by Théophile Gautier.

Le Train Bleu (restaurant) | Le Train Bleu ("The Blue Train") is a restaurant located in the hall of the Gare de Lyon railway station in Paris, France.  It was designated a "Monument Historique" in 1972.
response.summary_1: Evidence: passage states the ballet was "based on a scenario by Jean Cocteau." Bridge entity: Jean Cocteau. Missing fact: the title of Jean Cocteau's 1929 novel is not given in the passages. Retrieval directions: 1) Open Jean Cocteau's biography/bibliography (e.g., the Wikipedia "Jean Cocteau" page) and check the list of works for the entry published in 1929; 2) if needed, run a targeted query "Jean Cocteau 1929 novel" (site:wikipedia.org OR general web) to confirm the novel title. [[ ## completed ## ]]
response.hop2_query:
- site:en.wikipedia.org "Jean Cocteau" "works" 1929
- "Jean Cocteau 1929 novel"
- "Jean Cocteau Les Enfants terribles 1929" site:*
- Jean Cocteau bibliography 1929 novel
hop2_docs: Jean Cocteau | Jean Maurice Eugène Clément Cocteau (] ; 5 July 1889 – 11 October 1963) was a French writer, designer, playwright, artist and filmmaker.  Cocteau is best known for his novel "Les Enfants Terribles" (1929), and the films "The Blood of a Poet" (1930), "Les Parents Terribles" (1948), "Beauty and the Beast" (1946) and "Orpheus" (1949).  His circle of associates, friends and lovers included Kenneth Anger, Pablo Picasso, Gertrude Stein, Jean Hugo, Jean Marais, Henri Bernstein, Yul Brynner, Marlene Dietrich, Coco Chanel, Erik Satie, Albert Gleizes, Igor Stravinsky, Marie Laurencin, María Félix, Édith Piaf, Panama Al Brown, Colette, Jean Genet, and Raymond Radiguet.

Les Enfants Terribles (film) | Les Enfants Terribles ("The terrible children") is a 1950 French film directed by Jean-Pierre Melville and based on Jean Cocteau's novel of the same name.  The first feature film of Melville, "Le Silence de la Mer" (1949), attracted the attention of Jean Cocteau, who commissioned him to direct the film version of "Les Enfants Terribles".

Les Enfants Terribles | Les Enfants Terribles is a 1929 novel by Jean Cocteau, published by Editions Bernard Grasset.  It concerns two siblings, Elisabeth and Paul, who isolate themselves from the world as they grow up, an isolation which is shattered by the stresses of their adolescence.  It was first translated into English by Samuel Putnam in 1930 and published by Brewer & Warren Inc.  A later English translation was made by Rosamond Lehmann in 1955, and published by New Directions (ISBN  ) in the U.S., and Mclelland & Stewart in Canada in 1966, with the title translated as "The Holy Terrors".  The book is illustrated by the author's own drawings.

The Holy Innocents (Adair novel) | The Holy Innocents (1988) is a novel by Gilbert Adair about incestuous siblings and the stranger who enters their world.  Its themes were inspired by Jean Cocteau's novel "Les Enfants Terribles" ("The Holy Terrors") and by the film of the same name directed by Jean-Pierre Melville.

Marie-Laure de Noailles | Marie-Laure de Noailles, Vicomtesse de Noailles (] ) (31 October 1902 – 29 January 1970) was a French artist, regarded one of the 20th century's most daring and influential patrons of the arts, noted for her associations with Salvador Dalí, Balthus, Jean Cocteau, Ned Rorem, Man Ray, Luis Buñuel, Francis Poulenc, Wolfgang Paalen, Jean Hugo, Jean-Michel Frank and others as well as her tempestuous life and eccentric personality.  She and her husband financed Ray's film "Les Mystères du Château de Dé" (1929), Poulenc's "Aubade" (1929), Buñuel and Dalí's film "L'Âge d'Or" (1930), and Cocteau's "The Blood of a Poet" (1930).
Jean Cocteau bibliography | A list of books and essays about artist and filmmaker Jean Cocteau:

Jean Cocteau Museum | The Jean Cocteau Museum/Séverin Wunderman Collection is a museum in Menton, on the French Riviera, in the Alpes-Maritimes department.  Dedicated to the French artist Jean Cocteau, it incorporates the collection of American businessman and Cocteau enthusiast Séverin Wunderman.
response.summary_2: The 1929 novel was Les Enfants Terribles. Jean Cocteau — who wrote the scenario for the one-act ballet Le Train Bleu — is the author of that novel.
